articles.title: Great White Sharks in Trouble from Hot Water
articles.summary: Great white sharks are having a hard time because the ocean is getting warmer. These big sharks keep their bodies warmer than the water around them, which helps them swim fast and hunt. But as the water heats up from climate change, they can get too hot and might not survive. They also have less food to eat because of overfishing. Scientists studied sharks and found they need to move to cooler waters or slow down to stay safe. Sharks are important for the ocean because they help keep other animals in balance. If sharks disappear, it could hurt the whole ocean ecosystem. People are working to protect sharks by learning more about them and creating safe areas.

articles.title: Saving a Special Rainforest in Cumbria
articles.summary: In Cumbria, England, volunteers are working to save a rare type of rainforest called a temperate rainforest. This rainforest used to cover more of the UK but now is very small. The Friends of the Lake District group takes care of two woods in the Rusland Valley. They want to raise money to help the rainforest by planting trees, removing bad plants, and making spaces for wildlife. During Earth Week in April, donations will be doubled, so every pound given becomes two pounds. The rainforest is home to mosses, lichens, and butterflies. People like Marion Brown volunteer to protect it from threats like non-native species and climate change. The group hopes to expand their work to more land and create public spaces for everyone to enjoy.

articles.title: A Hidden Forest Under the Sea
articles.summary: Long ago, there was a land called Doggerland under the North Sea. Scientists found that forests grew there over 16,000 years ago, which is much earlier than they thought. These forests had trees like oak, elm, and hazel. Doggerland was a land bridge connecting Britain to Europe before it sank. The forests could have been a home for animals and maybe early humans. Researchers studied old DNA from mud samples to learn about this lost world. They discovered that some trees survived longer than expected. This helps us understand how plants and animals lived during the Ice Age. Doggerland might have been a safe place for life before it flooded.

articles.title: Glaciers That Move Fast and Cause Danger
articles.summary: Most glaciers are getting smaller because the Earth is getting warmer. But some glaciers, called surging glaciers, act differently and can be very dangerous. Scientists studied over 3,100 of these glaciers around the world. A glacier surge happens when ice that usually moves slowly suddenly speeds up, pushing lots of ice forward quickly. This can last for years and happen again and again. These glaciers are found in places like the Arctic, High Mountain Asia, and the Andes. They can cause floods and avalanches, which are big problems for people living nearby. Climate change is making these surges harder to predict, and they can lose a lot of ice during surges. Scientists are worried because this affects thousands of people, especially in mountainous areas where communities are close to the glaciers.

articles.title: A Chain of Atoms That Feels Electric Fields
articles.summary: Scientists have made a special chain of atoms that can feel electric fields very well. Electric fields are invisible forces that can push or pull things, like when you rub a balloon and it sticks to your hair. This new chain is made of Rydberg atoms, which are atoms that are very sensitive to electric fields. Instead of using a gas of atoms, the scientists put these atoms in a line, like beads on a string. When an electric field comes near, it changes how the atoms in the chain talk to each other. By watching how the atoms move and interact, the scientists can tell how strong the electric field is and which way it is pointing. This helps make better sensors that are small and accurate, which could be used in science and technology to measure things more precisely.

articles.title: Bacteria Explode to Share Superpowers
articles.summary: Scientists found that bacteria can explode to share genes that help them fight antibiotics. They studied tiny particles called gene transfer agents (GTAs), which are like delivery trucks for DNA. These GTAs pick up DNA from one bacterial cell and carry it to others, allowing bacteria to quickly get new abilities, such as resisting antibiotics. A key part of this process is when the bacterial cell breaks open, or explodes, to release the GTAs. Researchers discovered a system called LypABC that controls this explosion. Surprisingly, LypABC looks like a defense system against viruses, but bacteria now use it to help share genes. This helps explain how antibiotic resistance spreads, which is a big problem for health. The study was done by scientists at the John Innes Centre and other universities.
